meta.title: The AI-Lab Capability Race
meta.subject: Four labs, four years, and the gaps that shortened — the AI capability race 2022-2026

scenes.title: The AI-Lab Race
scenes.tagline: Four years, four labs — and the months between them shrank
scenes.footnote: A timeline reading of the frontier-model release record · 2022 through 2026 · the gaps are the argument

beats.narration: This is the AI-lab capability race -- not the labs, not the benchmarks, the gaps. The years between releases tell you something the releases themselves do not.
From late 2022 to the present we will plot four labs onto a real date axis -- and watch the spacing collapse.
The claim under test: this is not a steady drumbeat of progress. It is an accelerating one, and the dates prove it.
The axis runs from early 2022 to late 2026. It begins on November 30th, 2022: ChatGPT lands. Not a model release -- a public event. Everything after it is downstream of this one date.
Sixteen weeks later, GPT-4. The gap from the consumer surprise to the frontier model is one season.
Then Anthropic enters the public surface with Claude 2 in July, and by December Google answers with Gemini 1.0. Inside one year, four lab-level moves.
Two thousand twenty four is when the spacing collapses. Look: Claude 3 in March, Llama 3 a month later, GPT-4o the month after, o1 in September, Claude 3.5 in October. Five releases in seven months.
And in January twenty twenty five, an outsider: DeepSeek R1, with open weights, reasoning trained for a small fraction of the budget. The race stops being a four-lab race.

scenes.heading: Two eras on the same axis

beats.narration: Now look at the same axis with two bands beneath it. The slow era and the fast era.
The slow era runs roughly sixteen months: November twenty twenty two through Claude 3 in March twenty twenty four. The cadence is quarterly. A lab releases, a lab answers, the field catches up.
Then the fast era. From Claude 3 onward, the cadence becomes monthly. Same four labs, but the gap shrinks by a factor of three.
September twenty twenty four: o1. Reasoning trained into the model rather than prompted out of it. Three months later DeepSeek R1 reproduces the result with open weights.
The two bands are the argument. Same labs, same years, same axis -- and the gap shortens. You cannot say this with a list. The dates have to be real, and the spacing has to be visible.

scenes.heading: What shortened — and what it cost

beats.narration: So what actually shortened the gap? The labs chose RL on reasoning over more pre-training compute. That is the bet, and we can name what it costs.
Reasoning-style training is cheaper to reproduce than a 10x compute jump. The result: DeepSeek R1 closed the gap to o1 in three months. The labs traded a moat for a release calendar.
And the benchmarks the field used to measure progress -- MMLU, HumanEval -- saturated. We do not know whether the fast era is real capability or whether the eval ran out of headroom.

The residual risk: pre-release evaluation runs were compressed alongside the cadence. A monthly release calendar leaves weeks, not months, to red-team. Five billion dollar training runs, evaluated in weeks. That is the cost of the trade.

scenes.heading: The gaps are the argument
scenes.statement: The release calendar compressed three-fold because the labs traded a compute moat for reasoning RL.

beats.narration: Here is the line to leave with. The release calendar compressed three-fold -- because the labs traded a compute moat for reasoning RL.
Three times -- a real ratio on a real axis. The slow era was sixteen months, the fast era was five. The dates earn that number; the names alone cannot.

scenes.heading: Read the gaps, not the names
points: The release dates are real — the spacing carries the claim, not the labels.
Quarterly to monthly: a 3× cadence shift inside one year, the same labs.
Trade-off: reasoning RL replaced a compute moat — open weights caught up in months.
Residual risk: pre-release evals compressed alongside the calendar; multi-billion-dollar runs red-teamed in weeks.

beats.narration: Four labs. Four years. The names matter less than the gaps between them.
From late 2022 through early 2024 the cadence was quarterly. From Claude 3 onward, monthly. A three-fold compression with the same labs and -- mostly -- the same compute scale per release.
What shortened the gap was a method change: reasoning trained into the model. The cost of that change is a shorter moat -- DeepSeek R1 reproduced the result with open weights inside three months.
The skepticism to carry: a monthly release calendar leaves weeks, not months, to evaluate. The fast era looks like progress on a date axis. We do not yet know how well it would look on a safety one.
